food_subgroup: Squid and cephalopod dishes; spicy sour soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yam Sotong. FOODON_03302051 (squid dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Squid and cuttlefish, prepared) used as closest parent; no specific Tom Yam Sotong code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Tom Yam Sotong or equivalent squid soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Tom Yam Sotong is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one bowl of soup with squid pieces and broth as typically served at a hawker stall or casual restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Soup with Seafood — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; adapted for squid-dominant variant
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Tom Yam Sotong exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for squid (cooked, moist heat; FDC ID 175159) with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Tom Yum soup broth. Squid contributes approximately 60% of dish weight; broth and vegetables contribute 40%. Macros weighted accordingly. Sodium elevated due to fish sauce. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a typical hawker-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Seafood Dishes and Soups — analogous estimate based on squid (cooked) and Tom Yum broth components; University of Sydney GI Database reference for seafood and low-carbohydrate soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yam Sotong. GI estimated at 35 based on: (1) cooked squid has a very low GI due to high protein and negligible starch content; (2) Tom Yum broth is predominantly water, aromatics, and fish sauce with minimal glycemic carbohydrates; (3) University of Sydney GI Database lists similar low-carbohydrate seafood soups in the GI 30–40 range. GL per serving = (4.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350 g serving / 100) × 35 / 100 = 5.1 g. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Tom Yam Sotong contains approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per standard 350 g bowl), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 35 and only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Tom Yam Sotong has a low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the sodium content from fish sauce warrants monitoring for those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from squid
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid contributes approximately 145 mg cholesterol per 100 g, and the dish is high in sodium from fish sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency, and consider requesting a reduced-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yam Sotong is a spicy and sour squid soup that is popular in Singapore and Malaysia, drawing on Thai culinary traditions. The dish features tender pieces of squid (sotong in Malay) simmered in a fragrant broth made from lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chillies, fish sauce, and lime juice, often with mushrooms, tomatoes, and shallots. The result is a bold, aromatic soup with a characteristic hot-sour flavour profile. The squid provides a firm yet slightly chewy texture that contrasts with the light, intensely flavoured broth. Tom Yam Sotong is commonly served as a main dish at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and casual restaurants across Singapore, often accompanied by steamed white rice or as part of a shared meal. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein from squid, low in carbohydrates, and has a low glycemic index, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and is a concern for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally dairy-free and gluten-free, and is halal when prepared with compliant fish sauce. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus from the squid.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Tom Yam Sotong?
Is Tom Yam Sotong suitable for diabetics?
Is Tom Yam Sotong high in sodium?
Tom Yam Sotong vs Tom Yam Kung
Can I eat Tom Yam Sotong if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: part of a multi-dish shared meal

ingredient_synonyms.sotong: squid, cuttlefish (colloquial Malay/Singaporean term for squid)
ingredient_synonyms.tom yam: tom yum, Thai spicy sour soup base
ingredient_synonyms.kaffir lime leaves: makrut lime leaves, daun limau purut (Malay)
ingredient_synonyms.fish sauce: nam pla (Thai), patis (Filipino), ikan bilis sauce

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Tom Yam Sotong was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked squid with FAO/INFOODS SEA Tom Yum broth composition data. GI was estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar low-carbohydrate seafood soups. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine classification: The input JSON incorrectly tagged this dish as 'Indian' cuisine and 'Grains and staples' food group. Tom Yam Sotong is a Thai-origin dish widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia; it is a seafood soup, not a grain or staple. These fields have been corrected. 2. Nutrient estimation: Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (cooked squid, FDC ID 175159) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Tom Yum broth data. No exact match was found in any primary regional source. 3. Sodium: The high sodium value (480 mg/100g) reflects typical fish sauce usage in hawker-style preparation; actual values may vary significantly by restaurant. 4. Cholesterol: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (~145 mg/100g cooked); this is reflected in the per_100g values. 5. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg = -1): Not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked squid or in FAO/INFOODS SEA broth data; sentinel value -1 used. 6. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg = -1): Not reported in the primary sources used for this estimation; sentinel value -1 used. 7. Trans fat (trans_fat_g = 0.0): Confirmed zero — squid and plant-based aromatics do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. GI: Estimated at 35 (low) by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database seafood soup entries; no direct GI measurement for Tom Yam Sotong was found. GL per 350g serving = (4.2 × 350 / 100) × 35 / 100 = 5.1. 9. Multilingual naming: Thai script (ต้มยำหมึก) included as this is a Thai-origin dish widely served in Singapore. Mandatory Singaporean language coverage (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) also provided. 10. Restaurant source: Input listed 'Baba Eating House - South Bridge Road' (1 restaurant); this is a field survey seed record and does not constitute a nutritional source.
